issues.title: Toast notifications lack ARIA live region and role attributes
issues.description: The ToastProvider renders toast notifications without aria-live='polite', role='status', or role='alert' attributes. Screen readers never announce toast messages. Global acceptance criteria requires all status changes announced via aria-live regions.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/ContextMenu.tsx, in the ToastEntry component: find the outermost container div rendered for each toast. BEFORE: The toast container div has no ARIA attributes, so screen readers ignore toast announcements entirely. AFTER: Add role='status' and aria-live='polite' to the toast container div for success/info/warning toasts. For error severity toasts, use role='alert' and aria-live='assertive' instead. Also add aria-atomic='true' to ensure the full message is read. The dismiss button inside each toast must have aria-label='Dismiss notification'. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Breadcrumbs component has incomplete mobile responsive logic with truncated code
issues.description: The FolderBreadcrumbs component starts a useState for isMobile detection but the code appears truncated. The crumbButtonStyle has minHeight/minWidth of 44px (good for touch targets) but the responsive breakpoint logic is incomplete, likely causing rendering issues on mobile devices under 768px.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/Breadcrumbs.tsx, in the FolderBreadcrumbs component: find the useState declaration for isMobile. BEFORE: The isMobile state initialization uses an arrow function that appears truncated or incomplete, potentially causing a runtime error on mobile. The component may not properly collapse breadcrumbs on small screens. AFTER: Complete the isMobile useState to check window.innerWidth < 768. Add a useEffect that listens to the window resize event (debounced at 200ms) and updates isMobile. When isMobile is true, only show the last breadcrumb segment with a back arrow button instead of the full path, ensuring the breadcrumb trail fits within 375px width. Each clickable breadcrumb must maintain the existing 44px minimum touch target. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Toast error messages never auto-dismiss, creating zombie toasts with broken countdown
issues.description: Error toasts have TOAST_DURATION set to null (never auto-dismiss) but a countdown state initialized to 60. The countdown interval logic and fallback timer refs suggest a 60 second fallback was intended but the implementation is incomplete in the visible code. Error toasts may accumulate and cover the screen.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/ContextMenu.tsx, in the ToastEntry component: find the useEffect that handles the countdown for error toasts and the fallbackTimerRef logic. BEFORE: Error toasts set TOAST_DURATION to null and initialize countdown to 60, but the fallback auto-dismiss after 60 seconds is incomplete. Error toasts may pile up permanently. AFTER: For error severity toasts, implement a 60 second countdown that decrements every second via countdownIntervalRef. Display the countdown as a small text '(Xs)' next to the dismiss button. When countdown reaches 0, call handleClose. For non-error toasts, show a smooth progress bar that shrinks from 100% to 0% over the toast duration using progressPct and progressTransitionDuration (which are already declared as state). Clear all intervals and timeouts on unmount. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: No global skeleton loader component following design standards
issues.description: The design standards mandate skeleton loaders matching final layout geometry with pulse animation (opacity 0.3 to 0.7, 1.5s cycle). No reusable Skeleton component exists in Primitives.tsx. Pages either show blank content or generic spinners, violating the global acceptance criteria that every page must show a skeleton loader within 100ms.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/Primitives.tsx, add a new exported Skeleton component at the end of the file. BEFORE: No Skeleton component exists. Pages that need loading states have no standardized skeleton to use. AFTER: Create and export a Skeleton component that accepts width (string, default '100%'), height (string, default '20px'), borderRadius (string, default '8px'), and variant ('text' | 'rect' | 'circle', default 'rect') props. The component renders a div with background color '#E5E7EB', the specified dimensions, and a CSS keyframe pulse animation that oscillates opacity between 0.3 and 0.7 over 1.5 seconds infinitely. For variant 'circle', set borderRadius to '50%'. For variant 'text', set height to '14px' and borderRadius to '4px'. Also export a SkeletonRow component that renders 4 Skeleton text elements stacked with 8px gap (for table row placeholders), and a SkeletonCard component that renders a Card with 24px padding containing a Skeleton rect (height 28px, width 60%), a Skeleton text (width 80%), and a Skeleton text (width 40%) stacked with 12px gap. Use inline styles only with theme tokens. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: No standardized empty state component enforcing contextual messaging
issues.description: Global acceptance criteria requires every page to have a designed empty state with contextual illustration/icon, single-sentence explanation, and one primary CTA. No reusable EmptyState component exists in Primitives. Pages either show nothing or generic 'No data found' text, which is explicitly prohibited.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/Primitives.tsx, add a new exported EmptyState component at the end of the file. BEFORE: No EmptyState component exists. Pages have inconsistent or missing empty states, violating the global standard that says never show 'No data found'. AFTER: Create and export an EmptyState component that accepts: icon (React.ReactNode, required), title (string, required, e.g. 'No RFIs yet'), description (string, required, e.g. 'When questions arise in the field, create an RFI to get a documented answer'), actionLabel (string, optional, e.g. 'Create First RFI'), onAction (function, optional), and secondaryActionLabel/onSecondaryAction (optional). Render a centered flex column container with max-width 420px, 48px padding. The icon renders at 48px size in colors.textTertiary. Title uses 18px semibold in colors.textPrimary. Description uses 14px regular in colors.textSecondary with 1.6 line height. The primary action renders as a Btn variant='primary' and the secondary as a Btn variant='ghost'. Spacing: 16px between icon and title, 8px between title and description, 24px between description and action buttons. Use inline styles with theme tokens only. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore's UI design system includes a comprehensive component library (Procore Design System) with documented skeleton loaders, toast notifications with full ARIA support, standardized empty states per module, and responsive breadcrumbs that collapse to a back button on mobile. Fieldwire's mobile-first approach uses a bottom sheet pattern for all toasts with haptic feedback. SiteSync's design system primitives exist but lack the polish layer: no skeleton components, incomplete toast accessibility, no standardized empty state, and broken mobile breadcrumbs. These are foundational gaps that every page inherits.
summary: The UI design system has solid foundations with a theme token system, Card/MetricBox/Btn/Tag primitives, and a toast notification system with severity levels. However, critical gaps remain in accessibility (toasts lack ARIA live regions), mobile responsiveness (breadcrumbs component has truncated responsive logic), loading states (no reusable Skeleton component despite design standards mandating them on every page), and empty states (no standardized EmptyState component, leading to generic or missing empty states across all pages). The toast system has a potential zombie toast bug where error notifications may never auto-dismiss. These five issues are foundational because every page in the app depends on these shared components. Fixing them will cascade improvements across all 15+ pages.
